name: Singapore
overview: Singapore's Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (PDPA) governs private-sector processing of personal data, built around consent with broad 'deemed consent' and legitimate-interests exceptions added in the 2020 amendments, which also introduced mandatory breach notification and data portability provisions. The PDPC is an active enforcer and a leading voice on AI governance in Asia.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection Act 2012
legal_instruments.summary: Baseline private-sector data protection law: consent (including deemed consent), purpose limitation, protection, retention and transfer limitation obligations, Do Not Call registry, and mandatory breach notification (since Feb 2021). Fines up to 10% of Singapore turnover.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection Regulations 2021
legal_instruments.summary: Implementing regulations covering overseas transfer conditions (comparable protection via contract, BCRs, certification), access/correction procedures and prescribed breach notification detail.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection (Notification of Data Breaches) Regulations 2021
legal_instruments.summary: Prescribes notifiable breach thresholds: significant harm categories or breaches affecting 500 or more individuals; notify PDPC within 3 calendar days of assessment.

developments.title: PDPC issues advisory guidelines on AI recommendation systems
developments.summary: Guidelines clarify how the PDPA's consent and notification obligations apply to personal data used in AI systems that make recommendations or decisions.

developments.title: Health Information Bill consultation advances sectoral data-sharing rules
developments.summary: Singapore progressed sector-specific health information legislation to complement the PDPA for national health records sharing.

penalties: Up to 10% of annual turnover in Singapore (for organisations with local turnover above SGD 10 million) or SGD 1 million, whichever is higher.
cross_border: Transfers allowed where the recipient is bound to a comparable standard of protection (contractual clauses, binding corporate rules, APEC CBPR certification, or specified adequacy).
